faq.q: How much does it cost to start a salvage yard?
faq.a: Plan for $50,000-$75,000 in personal capital plus SBA financing. Total first-year costs typically range from $75,000 to $200,000 depending on property, equipment, and initial vehicle inventory. SBA 7(a) loans are the most common financing path for new auto dismantler operations.

faq.q: Do you need a special license to be an auto dismantler?
faq.a: Yes. Every state requires a dismantler or salvage dealer license from the DMV or equivalent agency. You'll also need environmental permits, a business license, and a surety bond ranging from $10,000 to $50,000. California's bond requirement is $50,000 and Texas requires $25,000.

faq.q: How do salvage yards find parts they don't have in stock?
faq.a: Yards use voice hotline networks, online databases, and direct calls to other dismantlers. On the Hotline HQ network, one broadcast reaches 100+ yards simultaneously with an average 2-second response time. Online databases like Car-Part.com offer broader but slower searches.

posts.bodyHtml:
Here's the deal with the auto dismantler business. You buy end-of-life vehicles, tear them apart, and sell the parts for more than the whole car cost you. The Automotive Recyclers Association (ARA) estimates US yards process over 12 million vehicles annually, feeding a $32 billion used parts market (ARA, 2025). That number isn't shrinking. Average vehicle age hit 12.6 years in 2024, per S&P Global Mobility, and that keeps pushing demand for replacement parts dealers don't stock new anymore.

This guide covers how the business works from end to end. You'll learn how yards source vehicles, dismantle them, manage inventory, and sell parts through channels including auto parts hotline networks. If you're thinking about opening a yard, or looking to sharpen operations at one you already run, every section here is built on real numbers and industry experience.

Key Takeaways
>
- US auto recyclers process 12M+ vehicles yearly, feeding a $32B parts market (ARA, 2025) - Well-run yards can reach profitability within 12 months on $50K-$75K plus SBA financing - Five sales channels: voice hotlines, online marketplaces, walk-ins, social media, locating services - Licensing requirements vary by state but always include a dismantler license and environmental permits

What Does an Auto Dismantler Business Do?

An auto dismantler buys end-of-life vehicles, pulls the usable parts, and sells them at a fraction of new OEM prices. The industry employs over 140,000 workers across roughly 9,000 facilities nationwide, per the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS, 2024). It's one of the oldest recycling industries in the country, and one of the most profitable per square foot of yard space.

The core model is dead simple. Buy a car for $300-$800 at auction or from a tow-in. Strip every sellable part off it: engine, transmission, body panels, lights, electronics, interior trim. Sell those parts individually for a combined total that's 3x to 5x the vehicle cost. Crush the leftover shell and sell the scrap metal.

What separates profitable yards from the ones barely hanging on isn't the model. It's execution. Yards that move inventory fast, price accurately, and sell through multiple channels crush yards that rely on walk-in traffic alone. How much can you squeeze from each vehicle? That single question drives every decision in this business.

[IMAGE: Aerial view of an organized salvage yard with rows of vehicles sorted by make and model - search terms: salvage yard aerial organized auto dismantler facility]

How Do Salvage Yards Source Vehicles?

Salvage yards pull in vehicles through four main channels: insurance auctions, public auctions, direct tow-ins, and private purchases. Insurance auto auctions supply roughly 40% of vehicles entering the recycling stream, according to the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS, 2024). The cost profile and part quality shift depending on which channel you're buying from.

Insurance Auctions

Insurance companies total out vehicles when repair costs exceed the car's value. Those cars go to auction through platforms like Copart and IAA. This is the most consistent supply channel for dismantlers. Expect to pay $200-$1,500 per vehicle depending on make, model, year, and damage type. Late-model vehicles with minor damage run higher because their parts move faster and pull bigger margins.

Public Auctions and Government Sales

County and city governments auction off abandoned, seized, and forfeited vehicles on a regular schedule. Prices tend to run lower, but vehicle condition is a coin flip. Some yards pick up 5-10 vehicles per month from these sales alone. Do your homework before bidding. Inspect every vehicle you can.

Tow-Ins and Private Purchases

Plenty of yards keep standing relationships with tow companies and body shops. When a car isn't worth fixing, the shop calls a dismantler who hauls it away for $100-$500. Some yards also buy directly from individuals through online classifieds or yard signage. These vehicles often cost less than auction buys, but volume is harder to predict.

[CHART: Bar chart - Average vehicle acquisition cost by channel (insurance auction, public auction, tow-in, private purchase) - source: ARA industry surveys and Hotline HQ network data]

From Whole Car to Sellable Parts: The Dismantling Process

A good dismantler can process a vehicle in 2-4 hours, pulling 200-400 sellable parts per car. The Automotive Recyclers Association reports that roughly 80% of a scrapped vehicle's weight gets recycled (ARA, 2025). The process follows a strict sequence designed to maximize part value and keep contamination out of the equation.

Fluid Removal

Every vehicle starts with fluid drainage. Engine oil, transmission fluid, coolant, brake fluid, power steering fluid, refrigerant, and fuel all come out first. No shortcuts here. EPA regulations require proper capture and disposal of all automotive fluids. Most yards run dedicated drain stations with built-in containment and separate tanks for each fluid type.

Part Removal and Testing

After draining, high-value components come off first: engines, transmissions, alternators, starters, and electronic modules. Every part gets inspected and tested where possible. Engines may get compression-tested. Transmissions get checked for obvious damage. Electronics get plugged in to verify function before they go on the shelf.

Body panels, lights, mirrors, and interior pieces come next. Order matters here. Pulling a door before the fender is easier. Getting the dashboard out before the seats gives you room to work. Speed counts, but so does care. A scratched bumper sells for less than a clean one.

What Remains

After the teardown, the stripped shell goes to a crusher. Crushed vehicles get sold to scrap metal processors at whatever the market rate is that week. Scrap typically adds $150-$300 per vehicle to the bottom line. In a well-run shop, nothing goes to waste.

[IMAGE: Auto dismantler removing an engine from a vehicle on a lift in an organized work bay - search terms: auto dismantler engine pull salvage yard shop mechanic]

How Do Yards Manage Parts Inventory?

Inventory management is what separates profitable yards from chaotic ones. A mid-size yard carries 5,000-15,000 parts at any given time, per ARA industry surveys (2025). Tracking what you have, where it sits, and what it's worth takes a real system. Relying on memory and handwritten tags doesn't cut it anymore.

Tagging and Cataloging

Every part pulled gets tagged with a unique identifier, vehicle source (year, make, model, VIN), condition grade, and storage location. Most yards run barcode or QR code systems tied to inventory software. Popular platforms include Pinnacle, Checkmate, and Hollander. The right salvage yard software pays for itself within months through faster lookups and fewer lost parts.

Storage and Organization

How you store parts affects how fast you sell them. Engines and transmissions go on racks, sorted by vehicle type. Body panels stand in organized rows. Small parts like alternators, starters, and sensors go into labeled bins. Walk-in customers need to find parts fast. Your crew needs to pull parts for phone and online orders even faster.

Inventory Turnover

Parts sitting 12+ months are costing you money in storage space and tied-up capital. Track what sells and what doesn't. If a part hasn't moved in 90 days, reprice it. At 180 days, it might be worth more as scrap than the shelf space it's eating. Why let capital collect dust? Smart yards watch turnover rates monthly and adjust pricing accordingly.

[CHART: Pie chart - Typical salvage yard inventory breakdown by category (engine/drivetrain, body panels, electrical, interior, suspension, misc) - source: ARA industry surveys]

Five Channels for Selling Salvage Parts

Successful yards don't rely on one sales pipeline. The US used auto parts market hit $32 billion in 2025 according to ARA, and that demand spreads across five distinct channels. Yards running all five capture revenue they'd flat-out miss otherwise.

Voice Hotlines

Live voice networks let you hear part requests from dozens or hundreds of yards in real time. Another yard needs a 2014 Ford F-150 tailgate? You hear the request on your desk phone and respond in seconds. The Hotline HQ network averages a 2-second response time across 500+ member yards in 12 regional rooms (Hotline HQ network data, 2026). For a deeper look, see our guide on how auto parts hotline networks work.

Online Marketplaces

Car-Part.com, eBay Motors, and Facebook Marketplace give you access to retail buyers nationwide. Listing parts online extends your reach well beyond your region. The trade-off: inventory data goes stale fast. A part you sold yesterday might still show available online today. Keeping listings current takes daily attention.

Walk-In Traffic

Local mechanics, body shops, and DIY car owners still walk into yards looking for parts. Walk-in customers typically pay full retail, making this your highest-margin channel. Good signage, a clean yard, and helpful staff keep them coming back.

Social Media

Facebook groups and Instagram work well for specialty and high-demand parts. Post a rare JDM engine or a clean set of OEM wheels and you can drum up interest within hours. Social selling means consistent posting, but the cost is effectively zero.

Locating Services

Third-party locating services connect buyers with yards for a fee or commission. These make the most sense for hard-to-find parts where the margin justifies the cost. Some services charge the buyer; others take a cut from the seller.

For a full breakdown of how to maximize each channel, read our guide on selling more parts.

[IMAGE: Salvage yard office with a desk phone connected to a voice parts network and a computer showing online marketplace listings - search terms: salvage yard office desk phone computer parts business]

What Licenses and Permits Do You Need?

Every state requires specific licenses to operate as an auto dismantler. According to the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL, 2025), all 50 states regulate salvage vehicle operations, though the requirements look different everywhere. Count on needing a dismantler license, a general business license, and environmental permits at bare minimum.

Dismantler or Salvage Dealer License

Your state's DMV or equivalent agency issues this one. Applications typically require proof of a physical location, zoning approval, a surety bond ($10,000-$50,000 depending on state), and sometimes a background check. California's dismantler license calls for a $50,000 bond. Texas requires $25,000. Processing times run 30 to 90 days.

Environmental Permits

Handling automotive fluids, batteries, refrigerants, and tires puts you under EPA and state environmental agency oversight. You'll likely need a stormwater permit, a hazardous waste generator ID, and possibly an air quality permit if you run a crusher. Your state environmental agency's small business assistance program can walk you through the specifics for your location.

Zoning and Local Permits

Salvage yards run into zoning restrictions in most municipalities. You'll need property zoned for industrial or heavy commercial use. Some areas require buffer zones between yards and residential property. Building permits, fire department inspections, and signage permits may stack on top. Talk to your county planning department early. Zoning problems are the number-one reason new yard applications stall.

[CHART: Table - Licensing requirements comparison for CA, TX, FL, and AZ showing bond amounts, license types, and processing times - source: state DMV websites (2025)]

Environmental Compliance for Auto Recyclers

Environmental compliance isn't a paperwork exercise you do once and forget. It's an ongoing operational cost that protects your business and your neighbors. The EPA identifies improper automotive fluid handling as a leading source of groundwater contamination at small industrial sites (EPA, 2024). Proper fluid management, waste disposal, and stormwater controls aren't optional. Full stop.

Fluid Management

The average end-of-life vehicle holds 10-15 gallons of combined fluids: oil, coolant, transmission fluid, brake fluid, power steering fluid, gasoline, and refrigerant. Every one of them needs specific handling. Used oil and coolant can often be recycled through certified haulers. Refrigerant (R-134a or R-1234yf) must be recovered by a certified technician using EPA-approved equipment. Gasoline typically gets reused in yard vehicles or sold to fuel recyclers.

Hazardous Materials

Lead-acid batteries, mercury switches, and catalytic converters all need special handling. Batteries go to certified recyclers. Mercury switches found in trunk lights and ABS sensors on older vehicles must be removed before crushing per federal law. Cats carry value because of their precious metal content, but theft concerns mean proper documentation on every unit is a must.

Stormwater and Containment

Rain running through a yard picks up oil, metal particles, and other contaminants. Most states require a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP). That means covered fluid-handling areas, paved and bermed storage zones, and regular inspections. One spill incident can cost more in fines and cleanup than years of compliance investment. Worth the upfront spend? Every single time.

[IMAGE: Properly covered fluid drainage and containment station at a well-organized auto recycling facility with labeled tanks - search terms: auto recycler fluid containment station environmental compliance]

What Does It Cost to Start a Salvage Yard?

Starting an auto dismantler business takes real money, but the barrier is lower than most people think. The Small Business Administration (SBA) notes that small recycling businesses typically launch with $50,000-$75,000 in owner equity plus additional financing. A well-managed yard can reach profitability within its first 12 months of operation.

Startup Cost Breakdown

Here's where the money goes:

  • Land or lease: $2,000-$10,000/month depending on location and acreage. Rural properties cost less but draw less walk-in traffic.
  • Equipment: Forklift ($15,000-$30,000), fluid evacuation system ($5,000-$10,000), engine hoist, hand tools, and storage racks ($10,000-$20,000 combined).
  • Initial vehicle inventory: 20-50 vehicles at $300-$1,500 each ($6,000-$75,000).
  • Licensing, bonds, and permits: $5,000-$15,000 depending on state.
  • Inventory software: $100-$500/month.
  • Insurance: $3,000-$8,000/year for general liability, property, and environmental coverage.

Total first-year capital need runs $75,000 to $200,000. The exact figure depends on whether you buy or lease property and how aggressively you stock initial inventory.

Financing and Profitability

Most yards piece together personal savings with SBA financing. SBA 7(a) loans cover up to $5 million with terms up to 25 years for real estate. Some dismantlers start lean: lease property, buy 10-20 vehicles, then plow revenue back in to grow. Cash flow management is everything during those first six months while you're building inventory and customer relationships at the same time.

[CHART: Stacked bar chart - Auto dismantler startup cost breakdown by category (land, equipment, vehicles, licensing, software, insurance) - source: SBA guidelines and ARA industry surveys]

Plug Into a Regional Network

A dismantler business grows faster when buyers come to you. Hotline HQ operates live voice rooms across California, Texas, Florida, and Arizona, with a web dashboard for tracking every request that touches your yard.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to start a salvage yard?

Plan for $50,000-$75,000 of your own capital plus SBA financing. Total first-year costs typically run $75,000-$200,000 depending on property, equipment, and initial vehicle inventory. The SBA's 7(a) loan program is the most common financing path for new auto dismantler operations.

Do you need a special license to be an auto dismantler?

Yes. Every state requires a dismantler or salvage dealer license from the DMV or equivalent agency. You'll also need environmental permits, a business license, and a surety bond. Bond amounts range from $10,000 to $50,000 by state. California's is $50,000; Texas is $25,000.

How do salvage yards find parts they don't have in stock?

Yards use voice hotline networks, online databases, and direct calls to other dismantlers. On the Hotline HQ network, a single broadcast reaches 100+ yards at once with an average response time of about 2 seconds. Online databases like Car-Part.com offer broader reach but slower, less current results.

---

Look, this business hasn't changed at its core in 50 years. Buy smart. Pull clean. Sell through every channel you can reach. The yards making real money aren't doing anything exotic. They're just doing the basics faster and more consistently than everyone around them. Get your systems right, connect with buyers, and the numbers take care of themselves.

If you're ready to add a live voice network to your sales mix, see how Hotline HQ works for yard owners.

posts.bodyHtml:
You've got five ways to find used auto parts online, and none of them does everything well. Car-Part.com alone indexes over 200 million parts (Car-Part.com, 2025). Voice networks get you confirmed answers in about 2 seconds. Databases cast the widest net. Voice hotlines give you the fastest confirmation. Facebook groups cost nothing but eat your time. Calling yards one by one still works. It's just slow.

This guide puts every approach side by side: online inventory databases, voice hotlines, social media groups, direct yard calls, and pull-a-part lots. We compare each on speed, accuracy, cost, and data freshness. For a deeper look at voice-based parts sourcing, read our guide on how auto parts hotlines work.

Key Takeaways
>
- Five methods exist for finding used auto parts online, from databases to voice hotlines - Car-Part.com offers the widest reach with 200M+ parts (Car-Part.com, 2025), but data freshness varies - Voice hotlines deliver the fastest confirmed responses at ~2 seconds per request - Combining two or three methods covers both breadth and speed

Five Ways to Find Used Auto Parts Online

Roughly 9,000 auto recycling facilities operate across the US, per the Automotive Recyclers Association (ARA, 2024). No single search method touches all of them. Your five options: online inventory databases, voice hotlines, Facebook groups, calling yards directly, and self-service pull-a-part lots.

Every option has a weak spot. Databases cast the widest net but serve stale data. Voice hotlines give you real-time answers but cover fewer total yards. Facebook groups are free but unreliable. Calling yards gets you a live person, one yard at a time. Pull-a-part lots deliver the cheapest prices if you're willing to wrench it yourself.

What matters most to your shop: speed, price, breadth, or knowing the part actually exists right now? Most yards we've talked to end up running two or three of these methods together.

[IMAGE: Salvage yard with rows of vehicles and organized parts shelving - salvage yard auto parts inventory rows]

Citation capsule: Approximately 9,000 auto recycling facilities operate across the United States, according to the Automotive Recyclers Association (ARA, 2024). No single parts-finding method reaches them all, making a multi-channel sourcing approach the practical standard for yards that locate parts regularly.

How Do Online Inventory Databases Compare?

Car-Part.com is the biggest online salvage parts database, indexing over 200 million parts from recyclers across North America (Car-Part.com, 2025). It's where most people start. With that kind of volume, you'll pull results for nearly any year, make, and model.

How database search works

You punch in the year, make, model, and part name. The database spits back matching listings from yards that uploaded that part to their inventory system. You see the yard name, location, price, and sometimes a photo. Then you call or email to confirm it's still on the shelf.

PartsHotlines.com takes a similar approach, searching a database of 1,000+ yards (PartsHotlines, 2025). eBay Motors and LKQ's online inventory do the same thing with different yard networks.

The freshness problem

This is the catch. Inventory data is only as current as the last upload. Some yards update daily. Others go weekly or monthly. A part listed on Monday may be sold by Wednesday, but the listing sits there until the next sync. You won't know until you call.

[UNIQUE INSIGHT] This freshness gap is the core limitation of every database approach. The larger the database, the more listings exist in a "maybe available" state. In our experience, breadth and freshness work against each other in practice.

For yards sourcing parts regularly, a database search is a solid starting point. Rarely the last step, though. You still pick up the phone and confirm. For more on how pricing works across these channels, see our used auto parts pricing guide.

Citation capsule: Car-Part.com indexes over 200 million salvage parts from recyclers across North America, making it the largest online parts database (Car-Part.com, 2025). PartsHotlines.com covers 1,000+ yards. Both offer broad search reach, but inventory freshness depends on how often individual yards upload their data.
[CHART: Bar chart - Inventory freshness by method: Voice hotline real-time, Direct call real-time, Pull-a-part real-time, Database days to weeks, Facebook hours to days - industry analysis]

How Do Voice Hotlines Find Parts Faster?

On the Hotline HQ network, a single broadcast reaches every yard in a regional room. Average response time: about 2 seconds (Hotline HQ network data, 2026). The reason is dead simple. You ask a live room full of people, and someone answers if they've got the part.

*Disclosure: Hotline HQ publishes this guide. We compare all methods honestly, including where others outperform us.*

How voice sourcing works

A yard keys up on their desk phone and describes what they need: year, make, model, part. Say a 2019 Honda Civic front bumper. Every connected yard in the regional room hears that request live. First yard with the part responds. The two yards work out the sale directly. No middleman.

Hotline HQ connects over 500 dismantler yards across 12 regional rooms (Hotline HQ network data, 2026). The four busiest rooms cover California, Texas, Arizona, and Florida. To see how this stacks up against other voice networks, read our parts network comparison.

Where hotlines fall short

Voice hotlines cover fewer total yards than a database like Car-Part.com. If you need a rare part from a region without active hotline coverage, a database search casts a wider net. Hotlines also require a monthly membership. You pay the same whether you broadcast once a day or a hundred times.

[ORIGINAL DATA] Across the Hotline HQ network, the California room alone has over 200 active yards. A single broadcast in that room reaches more yards instantly than most people could call in an entire day.

For common parts in active regions, nothing touches the speed. You're not searching a database that might be stale. You're asking real people who can walk their yard and confirm right now. Narrower reach, yes. But every answer is verified live.

Citation capsule: The Hotline HQ voice network connects over 500 salvage yards across 12 regional rooms in the US, delivering an average response time of approximately 2 seconds per broadcast (Hotline HQ network data, 2026). Voice hotlines trade database breadth for real-time accuracy and live confirmation.
[IMAGE: Desk phone on a salvage yard counter with parts shelves in background - desk phone auto parts salvage yard office]

Are Facebook Groups and Forums Worth Your Time?

Facebook Marketplace and parts-specific groups move millions of used auto parts listings every month (Meta, 2025). It costs nothing to post what you need and wait. For a consumer hunting one part, that can work fine. For yards sourcing daily, it's slow and a coin flip.

The trade-offs

Response times run hours to days. Listings aren't verified. You can't confirm part condition or availability without back-and-forth messages. The signal-to-noise ratio in big groups is brutal. You'll scroll past ten unrelated posts to find one that might match.

Still, Facebook groups sometimes turn up parts that don't show in any database. Small yards that never upload inventory will post parts in local groups. It's a side channel, not a primary sourcing tool.

What about dedicated forums or Reddit communities? Same trade-offs, smaller crowd. Useful for rare or specialty parts where patience pays off. But how many yards have time to watch three forums and four Facebook groups all day?

Citation capsule: Facebook Marketplace and auto parts industry groups handle millions of used parts listings monthly (Meta, 2025). Response times range from hours to days, and listings go unverified. Social channels function as supplementary sourcing for rare parts but lack the speed and reliability of dedicated parts networks.

What About Calling Yards Directly or Visiting Pull-a-Part Lots?

Calling yards one by one, a typical dismantler gets through 10 to 15 in 30 to 60 minutes (industry standard estimate). You get real-time confirmation. But the math falls apart fast. If you need to check 100 yards, that's a full day on the phone.

Calling yards directly

The upside is certainty. You talk to a person, they check their yard, you get a yes or no. No stale database entries. No waiting on a Facebook reply. The downside is time. Every call runs 2 to 5 minutes with hold time, and plenty go unanswered.

For a single hard-to-find part, calling targeted yards makes sense. You know which ones carry that make or model. You work the phone. For daily sourcing across a busy operation, it just doesn't hold up.

Pull-a-part and self-service yards

Pull-a-part yards like LKQ Pick Your Part and Pull-A-Part let you walk the lot and remove parts yourself. Prices typically run 40% to 60% below retail salvage yard prices. What's available depends entirely on whatever vehicles sit in the lot that week.

[PERSONAL EXPERIENCE] We've found that pull-a-part works best for consumers and small shops sourcing common parts from common vehicles. It's not practical for professional dismantlers sourcing specific year, make, and model parts under time pressure. You can't call ahead to check inventory at most self-service lots, so every visit is a gamble.

Citation capsule: Calling salvage yards individually reaches about 10 to 15 yards per hour, with real-time confirmation but poor scalability (industry estimate). Pull-a-part self-service yards offer the lowest prices, typically 40% to 60% below retail salvage, but require in-person visits with no guaranteed inventory.
[CHART: Comparison radar - Five methods rated 1 to 5 on Speed, Reach, Freshness, Cost, and Scalability - editorial analysis based on industry data]

Every Method Compared

All five methods, ranked on the factors that actually matter.

MethodSpeedReachData FreshnessCost
Voice hotlines~2 seconds100-500+ yards per broadcastReal-time (live confirmation)Flat monthly membership
Online databases5-15 minutes200M+ parts (Car-Part.com)Weekly to monthly updatesFree to search; subscription to list
Facebook groupsHours to daysGroup size dependentPost-dependentFree
Calling yards30-60 min for 10-15 yardsLimited by your timeReal-time (if they answer)Time cost only
Pull-a-part yardsVisit requiredLot inventory onlyReal-time (you're there)Per-part; 40-60% below retail
None of these wins across the board. Databases give you the most parts to search through. Voice hotlines get you the fastest confirmed answer. Calling yards gives you certainty but won't scale. Pull-a-part delivers the best price but needs your body there. Facebook is free but a crapshoot.

[UNIQUE INSIGHT] The most effective sourcing strategy combines two or three methods. Start with a database search to gauge availability and pricing. Then broadcast on a voice hotline for fast, confirmed responses in your region. Reserve direct calls for specialty parts where you know specific yards carry that make.

That way you're covered on both breadth and speed. Use each method where it's strong. Skip it where it's not.

Citation capsule: No single method for finding used auto parts online wins every category. Online databases offer the broadest reach with 200M+ listings. Voice hotlines deliver the fastest confirmed responses at approximately 2 seconds. Combining two or three methods gives professional yards the best coverage of both speed and breadth.

Which Method Fits Your Operation?

Think about how you source parts and how often. A dismantler fielding 20 customer calls a day needs speed and reliability. Period. A weekend mechanic chasing one part needs breadth and patience.

If you're a professional yard doing daily sourcing, pair a voice hotline membership with database access. Broadcast urgent requests on the hotline. Search databases for broader availability. That combination handles the bulk of sourcing without burning hours on the phone.

Searching once in a while? Car-Part.com and a few phone calls will get it done. Budget buyers willing to do the work should hit pull-a-part lots for the lowest cost per part.

Whatever you go with, the real question never changes: is the part actually available right now? Databases tell you it was available at some point. Voice hotlines and direct calls tell you it's there this second. When your customer is waiting, that gap matters.

For pricing guidance across all these channels, see our used auto parts pricing guide. For a detailed look at how voice networks compare to each other, check our parts network comparison.

Ready to try the fastest method? Find used auto parts on the Hotline HQ marketplace.

Find Parts in Your State

Hotline HQ runs live regional rooms where every member yard hears your request the moment you make it. The most active markets: used auto parts in California, Texas, Florida, and Arizona — Arizona especially for rust-free body panels. You can join from a desk phone, laptop, or any mobile device in minutes.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the fastest way to find a used auto part online?

Voice hotlines. Nothing else comes close. On the Hotline HQ network, a single broadcast hits 100+ yards at once. Average response time: about 2 seconds (Hotline HQ network data, 2026). Online databases beat calling yards one by one, but you still need to verify the part is sitting there before you commit.

Are online salvage parts databases accurate?

Depends on how often yards update their listings. Car-Part.com indexes over 200 million parts (Car-Part.com, 2025), but records can be days or weeks old. A part showing as available may already be gone. Voice hotlines close that gap by confirming availability in real time with a live person at the yard.

How much does it cost to use a parts-finding service?

It varies. Searching Car-Part.com is free for buyers. Voice hotline memberships like Hotline HQ charge a flat monthly fee, no per-call or commission charges. Facebook groups are free. Calling yards costs your time. Pull-a-part lots charge per part, typically 40% to 60% below retail salvage prices.

posts.bodyHtml:
What is an auto parts hotline?

The US automotive recycling industry employs over 140,000 people across more than 9,000 locations, generating $32 billion in annual sales (Automotive Recyclers Association, 2025). An auto parts hotline is how many of these yards find and sell parts to each other in real time, without typing a single search query or waiting on hold. If you're new to the industry, our auto dismantler business guide covers how the whole operation works end to end.

Key Takeaways - Voice hotlines broadcast part requests to 100+ yards simultaneously with ~2-second response times, replacing 30-60 minute phone call sessions - The US auto recycling industry processes 12-15 million end-of-life vehicles yearly across 9,000+ locations (ARA, 2025) - Hotline HQ operates 12 regional rooms connecting 500+ dismantler yards, with California's room exceeding 200 active members - Flat monthly pricing with no commissions keeps yard-to-yard transactions honest on high-value parts
Here's the basic idea. Members sit on an always-on conference room through a desk phone or web client. When a yard needs a specific part, say a 2019 Honda Civic front bumper, they key up and describe the ask. Every other yard in the room hears it instantly and responds if they've got it in stock.

This concept isn't new. Yards used radio networks and phone trees to locate parts for each other back in the 1990s. But radios were spotty. Coverage topped out at a few miles on a good day, and you couldn't reach yards two counties over.

Modern hotlines replaced all of that with internet-connected SIP phones delivering clear audio over dedicated conference bridges. The always-on model means yards don't need to call anyone. They just listen. When a request matches something sitting in their row, they answer. We've seen yards that were sitting on stale inventory for months suddenly move parts within hours of joining because they could finally hear the demand that was always there.

How does the voice network actually work?

Roughly 12 to 15 million vehicles reach the end of their useful lives annually in the United States, with about 26 automobiles recycled every minute (ARA, 2025). That's a massive flow of parts, and voice hotlines exist to match supply with demand in real time.

A yard joins a regional room. Could be California, Texas, Florida, Arizona, or one of eight other markets. Their desk phone connects to a conference bridge that runs around the clock. When a dismantler needs a part, three things happen:

  1. Broadcast. The requesting yard keys up and calls out what they need: year, make, model, and the specific part. That goes out live to every connected phone in the room. No typing. No waiting for search results.
  2. Listen. Every yard in the regional room hears the request through their desk phone speaker. Don't have it? Stay quiet. Simple as that.
  3. Respond. First yard with the part keys up and says so. The two yards connect directly to work out price, shipping, and pickup. No middleman sits between them.

The full cycle from broadcast to answer takes about 2 seconds on the Hotline HQ network. Compare that to calling yards one by one. That grind can eat 30 minutes to an hour just to reach 10 to 15 yards, and half of them won't pick up.

Our finding: On the Hotline HQ network, the average broadcast-to-response time is approximately 2 seconds across all 12 regional rooms, with the California room averaging 1.8 seconds due to its 200+ member density.

How do voice hotlines compare to other parts-finding methods?

The global automotive aftermarket was valued at $509.8 billion in 2026 (Grand View Research, 2026). Salvage yards compete for their share using several different methods, and each one trades off speed, reach, and data freshness differently.

MethodSpeedReachData freshnessCost model
Voice hotline (Hotline HQ)~2 seconds100+ yards per broadcastReal-time (live voice)Flat monthly
Calling yards individually30-60 minutes10-15 yards per hourReal-time (if they answer)Time cost
Online inventory databasesMinutesVaries by databaseStale (updated weekly/monthly)Per-listing or subscription
Facebook groups / forumsHours to daysGroup size dependentPost-dependentFree
Parts locating servicesHoursService networkDepends on servicePer-request or commission
Why does real-time matter so much? Inventory at a salvage yard changes daily as vehicles arrive and parts sell. A voice request asks a real person who can walk the row and confirm availability right now. That's fundamentally different from a database entry that was last updated Tuesday.

Online databases can technically search more yards. But stale data creates a frustrating pattern: you find the part listed, call the yard, and they sold it three days ago. Or worse, the listing was never accurate to begin with. Voice eliminates that lag entirely. For a deeper breakdown of every method, see our guide on how to find used auto parts online.

According to ARA data, approximately 86% of a vehicle's material content is recycled, reused, or recovered for energy (ARA, 2025). Voice hotlines help ensure that the remaining usable parts find buyers before they become scrap, maximizing the recovery value of every vehicle processed.

What makes a parts hotline actually effective?

Not all hotline networks produce equal results. After operating the Hotline HQ network for years, we've watched what separates a room that moves parts from one that collects dust on the counter.

  • Density of members per room. This is the single biggest factor. A room with 20 yards produces slim pickings. Pack 200 in and the hit rate jumps dramatically. Hotline HQ's California room has over 200 active yards, the highest density in any US voice parts network. There's a tipping point around 80 to 100 members where the network becomes self-sustaining because almost every request gets at least one response.
  • Always-on availability. Scheduled call windows don't work. Yards miss requests while they're on the lot pulling an engine. An always-on bridge means every ask reaches every connected yard, 24 hours a day, whether you're at your desk or across the property.
  • Regional organization. Shipping a bumper from California to Florida costs more than the part is worth half the time. Grouping yards by geography keeps requests matched with yards that can realistically deliver or arrange a local pickup.
  • Low friction to respond. If you've got to log into a website or type out a message, you've already lost the sale to whoever picked up the mic. Voice wins because a yard hears the request and keys up to say "I have it" in the same second.
  • No commission on sales. Networks that skim a percentage on every deal give yards a reason to work around the system on big-ticket parts. Flat monthly pricing keeps incentives aligned. You don't think twice about responding to a $2,000 transmission request because the network isn't taking a cut. See how Hotline HQ stacks up against other networks in our PartsHotlines vs Car-Part.com vs Hotline HQ comparison.

Who actually uses auto parts hotlines?

Dismantlers, salvage yards, and auto recyclers. These are businesses that buy end-of-life vehicles, break them down, and sell the usable parts. With around 9,000 locations operating across the US (ARA, 2025), they all face the same two problems every day.

As buyers: A customer calls asking for a part you don't carry. You've got two choices: lose the sale or find it somewhere else. On the hotline, you broadcast the request. Another yard in the region has it? You arrange a yard-to-yard sale and fulfill your customer's order. Sale saved. As sellers: You're sitting on parts nobody's asking about through your usual channels. By listening to the hotline, you hear every part request in your region. That transmission collecting dust for six months? Someone three counties over just asked for one. The hotline surfaces demand you'd never find through your website or walk-in traffic alone. For more on stacking sales channels, read how salvage yards sell more parts.

Why does it work? No two yards carry the same inventory mix. A yard in Los Angeles might have three Honda Civic transmissions while a yard in Sacramento has zero, and the reverse for Toyota Camry doors. Our most-requested used auto parts data shows exactly which parts and makes drive the most demand. The hotline turns a fragmented market of thousands of individual yards into one connected network where supply finds demand in seconds instead of days.

What does the Hotline HQ network cover?

Hotline HQ operates 12 regional rooms across the United States. The four busiest rooms, California, Arizona, Texas, and Florida, carry the bulk of daily broadcast activity. These four states account for a significant share of the nation's 9,000+ auto recycling locations, making them natural hubs for voice-based parts locating.

Each room runs independently with its own member base, keeping broadcasts relevant to yards that can realistically transact with each other. A California yard doesn't hear Florida requests, and vice versa. That geographic focus keeps the signal-to-noise ratio high and ensures responses lead to actual sales rather than logistically impossible deals.

How do you get started on a parts hotline?

Setup takes minutes, not days. Sign up online, pick your regional room, and a preconfigured Yealink desk phone ships to your yard. Plug it in and you're on the live room, hearing part requests in your region as they're broadcast.

There's no software to install, no inventory to upload, and no training period. If your crew can use a phone, they can use the hotline. The phone arrives ready to go with your room pre-programmed.

Most yards start hearing relevant requests within their first hour on the network. Whether you respond to those requests or just listen to understand the demand in your region, you're getting value from day one.

Check the Hotline HQ marketplace to see live requests and real pricing activity from the network.

faq.q: What is the fastest way to sell used auto parts?

faq.q: Can small salvage yards compete with large dismantlers on volume?

posts.bodyHtml:
Most salvage yards depend on one or two channels to move parts. That's a problem. The Automotive Recyclers Association estimates roughly 9,000 auto recycling businesses operate across the US, all chasing the same buyers. The yards that consistently clear shelves aren't the biggest. They're the ones plugged into more than one sales channel.

This guide ranks five channels by speed, reach, and real ROI. We cover voice hotlines, online marketplaces, walk-in traffic, social media, and parts locating services. Each solves a different problem. You don't need all five. Pick the two or three that fit your yard's size, location, and the kinds of most-requested used parts you actually stock.

*Disclosure: Hotline HQ publishes this guide and operates a voice hotline network (Channel 1 below). We've ranked all five channels honestly, including the trade-offs of our own.*

Key Takeaways
>
- Voice hotlines deliver the fastest response, about 2 seconds per broadcast (Hotline HQ network data) - Online marketplaces add national reach but charge fees and move slower - The most profitable yards stack two to three channels, not just one - Your best mix depends on yard size, region, and inventory type

Why Do Most Yards Rely on a Single Sales Channel?

A typical mid-size yard carries parts from 200 to 500 vehicles at any given time (ARA industry data). That's thousands of components on shelves. Most yards sell through walk-ins and repeat callers only. Parts sit for months while a buyer one county over never hears about them.

The fix isn't pulling more vehicles or adding more shelves. It's connecting what you already have to more buyers. A 2019 Honda Civic front bumper doesn't help anyone if only your regulars know it's there. Each channel below opens a different pipeline to people who'd buy from you today if they knew you existed.

For a broader look at how yards manage operations alongside sales, see our guide on dismantler business operations.

[IMAGE: Salvage yard with rows of organized vehicles and parts shelving - salvage yard inventory parts shelves organized]

How Do Voice Hotlines Help You Sell Faster?

Voice hotlines connect salvage yards on a live conference bridge, organized by region. On the Hotline HQ network, over 500 yards across 12 regional rooms hear every broadcast in real time (Hotline HQ network data). Average response time: about 2 seconds. Nothing else in this industry moves that fast.

The setup is dead simple. Your desk phone stays connected to a regional room, say California or Texas. Someone needs a part, they key up and describe it: "2016 Ford F-150 left headlight assembly." Every yard in the room hears the request live. You have the part? Key up and respond. The two yards arrange the sale directly. No middleman.

Compare that to 30 or 60 minutes calling yards one by one. On the hotline, a broadcast goes out and you've got an answer in seconds. For a deeper look at the model, read our guide on how a voice hotline network connects yards across every major market.

Pros:
  • Fastest time-to-sale of any channel (seconds, not hours)
  • One broadcast reaches 100+ yards at once
  • Flat monthly cost, no per-sale commission
  • Real-time means no stale inventory data
  • Two-way value: sell parts and source parts on the same channel
Cons:
  • Reach is limited to yards on your regional room
  • Requires someone near the phone during business hours
  • You respond to live requests rather than listing inventory for passive discovery
Best for: Yards doing steady daily volume that want the fastest path to a sale. The always-on model catches demand in the background while your team does other work.

[ORIGINAL DATA] Hotline HQ network data shows the California room alone has over 200 active yards, producing the highest broadcast density of any US voice parts network.

Online Marketplaces: Broader Reach, Slower Speed

Platforms like eBay Motors and Car-Part.com give dismantlers access to millions of buyers. Car-Part.com lists over 200 million parts from yards nationwide. No other single channel puts you in front of that many eyeballs. But broader reach comes with trade-offs: fees, stale data, and a slower sales cycle.

The downside with online listings: you photograph, describe, and price each part one at a time. Then you wait. A buyer has to search, find your listing, and complete the purchase. That takes hours to days. And because inventory updates are manual, buyers sometimes try to order parts you've already sold or pulled.

eBay charges roughly 13% to 15% on completed parts sales when you combine listing and payment processing fees (eBay Seller Center). Car-Part.com uses a subscription model instead. Either way, you're paying for reach.

Pros:
  • National buyer pool, millions of potential customers
  • Passive sales: listings work while you're closed
  • Strong for high-value, easily shipped parts (ECUs, headlights, sensors)
  • Buyers come pre-qualified, they searched for that exact part
Cons:
  • Fees eat 13-15% of every sale on eBay
  • Inventory data goes stale between manual updates
  • Time-intensive to photograph and list each part
  • Price competition across national sellers compresses margins
Best for: Yards with dedicated office staff to manage listings. Works well for specialty, rare, or high-value parts where national buyers will pay shipping and a premium.

[CHART: Bar chart - Average time from listing to sale by channel (voice hotline vs eBay vs Car-Part.com vs Facebook) - Hotline HQ analysis]

Walk-In Traffic: Reliable but Limited

Walk-in customers provide steady, predictable revenue at most yards. For many small-to-mid-size dismantlers, repeat local buyers account for 40% to 60% of total parts sales (figures vary by region and yard location). Highest-margin channel you've got. Zero fees, zero shipping costs. But it caps your buyer pool at roughly a 20 to 30 mile radius.

Walk-in volume depends on three things: location, signage, and local reputation. A yard on a busy road near body shops and mechanics draws traffic naturally. A yard tucked behind an industrial park? Not so much. You can improve numbers with better signage, a solid Google Business Profile, and word-of-mouth referrals. But there's a ceiling, and you'll hit it.

You'll never reach the body shop two states away that needs the exact part gathering dust on your shelf. Walk-in traffic works as a baseline. It can't be your only play.

[PERSONAL EXPERIENCE] Yards we've worked with that added even one additional channel, typically a voice hotline, saw parts that had sat for 60 to 90 days sell within the first two weeks after connecting.

Pros:
  • Zero fees, zero commissions, zero shipping costs
  • Cash transactions with fast payment
  • Builds long-term repeat customer relationships
  • Customers inspect parts in person, reducing returns
Cons:
  • Geographically limited to a 20-30 mile radius
  • Volume varies by weather, day of week, and season
  • Doesn't help move parts that local buyers aren't asking for
  • Hard to grow past the local market
Best for: Every yard benefits from walk-in traffic. It's your floor revenue. But relying on it alone means you're invisible to 99% of potential buyers.

[IMAGE: Customer at a salvage yard counter buying auto parts with shelves of parts behind the counter - salvage yard parts counter customer purchase]

Is Social Media Worth the Effort for Parts Sales?

Facebook Marketplace and local buy-sell groups have become a free sales channel for yards willing to post. Meta reports Facebook Marketplace reaches over 1 billion monthly visitors worldwide. Parts with clear photos sell, especially common items like engines, transmissions, and body panels. But results swing wildly from week to week.

The work itself isn't complicated. Post a photo with year, make, model, and price. Buyers message you directly. Some yards post 10 to 20 parts per week and see consistent inquiries. Others post the same volume and hear crickets. Facebook's algorithm decides who sees your listing. You don't control that, and it shifts without warning.

What about make-specific Facebook groups? They can work surprisingly well for enthusiast parts. A rare JDM engine or a classic Mustang fender gets more attention in a dedicated group than on any database. But the effort-to-return ratio is a coin flip.

Pros:
  • Free to list, no subscription or transaction fees
  • Reaches local consumers outside traditional yard networks
  • Make-specific groups attract serious, knowledgeable buyers
  • Photos and video help sell condition-sensitive parts
Cons:
  • Reach is entirely algorithm-dependent and changes without notice
  • Time-consuming to photograph, post, and respond to messages
  • Attracts tire-kickers, lowballers, and no-shows
  • No inventory management: sold parts stay posted until you remove them
  • Not designed for B2B yard-to-yard transactions
Best for: Yards with retail-friendly or specialty inventory and someone willing to manage posts consistently. Treat social media as a supplement, not a primary channel.

[UNIQUE INSIGHT] Social media works best for yards in metro areas where the local Facebook group population is large enough to generate real demand. Rural yards rarely see meaningful volume from this channel.

What About Parts Locating Services?

Parts locators act as middlemen between end buyers, usually repair shops, and salvage yards. Companies like PartsHotlines.com search databases of over 1,000 yards to match requests with available inventory. The locator handles the search and the customer relationship. But that convenience takes a real bite out of your margins. *(Note: Hotline HQ competes with parts locating services. We've tried to represent this channel fairly.)*

Most locators charge a per-request fee or take a commission on completed sales. Some pay you 30% to 40% below direct-sale prices. You're selling wholesale, plain and simple. The other issue is speed. Database-driven locating relies on inventory records that may be days or weeks old. By the time a locator matches your part to a buyer, a yard on a live network may have already closed that deal.

Pros:
  • Passive: the locator finds buyers for you
  • No listing, marketing, or sales effort on your end
  • Surfaces demand from repair shops that don't know your yard exists
Cons:
  • You sell at wholesale, typically 30-40% below direct prices
  • No relationship with the end customer
  • Dependent on the locator's network and effort
  • Stale database records can slow the match process
Best for: Moving slow-turning inventory that isn't selling through other channels. If a part has sat for 90 days with no matches on the hotline or your marketplace listings, a locator sale at a discount beats zero revenue.

Which Channels Should You Stack Together?

The most active sellers on the Hotline HQ network typically run two to three channels at once (Hotline HQ network data). No single channel covers all the demand for your parts. Here's a framework based on your biggest constraint.

ChannelSpeedReachCostMarginBest Use
Voice hotlineSecondsRegional (100+ yards)Flat monthlyHighDaily volume
Online marketplacesHours to daysNational (millions)13-15% feesMediumHigh-value parts
Walk-in trafficImmediateLocal (20-30 mi)FreeHighestBaseline revenue
Social mediaHours to daysVariableFreeHighSpecialty parts
Parts locatingHoursLocator's network30-40% discountLowSlow movers
Short on time? Hotline's the lowest-effort active channel. Plug in a phone, listen, respond when you've got the part. No listings, no photos, no algorithm games. Short on staff? Marketplaces work while the lights are off. Front-load the listing work, then let the platform field inquiries overnight. Short on budget? Walk-ins and Facebook cost nothing. They won't scale past your zip code, but they keep parts moving while you build up. The combination that works for most mid-size yards:
  1. Voice hotline as the primary channel for speed and daily volume
  2. Online marketplace (Car-Part.com or eBay) for national reach on high-value parts
  3. Walk-in traffic as the local baseline with the highest margins

Add Facebook for specialty or enthusiast parts when you've got them. Use locating services as a last resort for inventory that hasn't moved through faster channels.

The parts on your shelves aren't dead weight. They're unseen. Every one of them has a buyer somewhere. The only question is whether you're wired into enough channels to find that buyer before the part rusts out or the model ages off the road.

Ready to add a voice channel to your yard? See how Hotline HQ connects dismantlers to live buyers at the Hotline HQ marketplace.

Get on the Line in Your State

Every technique above works better with live buyer demand in your ear. Hotline HQ's busiest rooms are California, Texas, Florida, and Arizona — and unanswered request capture means you never miss a sale that was meant for you, even when the counter's slammed.

faq.q: Is PartsHotlines.com the same as Hotline HQ?
faq.a: No. PartsHotlines.com is a database-based search network where yards upload inventory and buyers search it online. Hotline HQ is a live voice network where yards join an always-on conference room and communicate in real time. The name sounds similar, but the technology and workflow are completely different.

faq.q: Can I use Car-Part.com and Hotline HQ at the same time?
faq.a: Yes, and many yards do. Car-Part.com gives you passive reach — your inventory is searchable by anyone, anytime. Hotline HQ gives you active, real-time reach — you hear live requests and respond in seconds. They complement each other well.

faq.q: Which used parts network has the fastest response time?
faq.a: Hotline HQ's live voice model delivers the fastest response. The average time from broadcast to first response on the Hotline HQ network is approximately 2 seconds. Database networks like Car-Part.com and PartsHotlines depend on how often inventory is updated and how quickly a seller responds to an inquiry.

posts.bodyHtml:
*Disclosure: Hotline HQ publishes this comparison. We've done our best to be fair and factual about all three networks. Where competitors do something better, we say so.*

Three networks, three models

If you run a salvage yard, three names keep coming up in parts network conversations: PartsHotlines.com, Car-Part.com, and Hotline HQ. Every yard owner hears about them sooner or later.

All three help you find and sell used auto parts. They just go about it very differently. PartsHotlines searches a curated database of member yards. Car-Part.com searches the largest inventory database in the industry. Hotline HQ skips databases entirely and connects yards on a live voice network.

Which one fits depends on what your yard needs most — reach, speed, or both. Here's how parts hotlines work at a fundamental level, and below is how these three stack up.

PartsHotlines.com

Founded: 1997 Network size: 1,000+ member yards Model: Database search with curated membership

PartsHotlines.com has been connecting yards for close to three decades. The model is simple: member yards upload inventory to a central database. A yard or buyer needs a part, they search PartsHotlines and get results from members that have it listed.

The network handles roughly 1.6 million searches per month. Yards are hand-selected for membership, so results tend to be higher quality than what you'd see from open-enrollment directories.

Strengths:
  • Large, established network with 1,000+ member yards
  • Hand-selected membership means results come from verified, operating yards
  • Nearly 30 years of industry relationships and brand recognition
  • 1.6 million monthly searches means real buyer traffic
  • Database search is familiar — works like any online parts lookup
Weaknesses:
  • Inventory data freshness depends on upload frequency. A part sold yesterday might still show as available today
  • Search results are only as good as the data yards submit. Incomplete uploads mean missed matches
  • Response isn't instant — a search returns listings, then you still need to call the yard and confirm
  • Buyer-seller communication happens outside the platform
Best for: Yards that maintain up-to-date inventory records and want passive exposure to a large, curated network of verified yards.

Car-Part.com

Network size: 200 million parts searchable Model: Massive inventory database with software integration

Car-Part.com is the largest used auto parts search engine in the industry. 200 million searchable parts — nothing else comes close in raw database size. The platform pulls inventory data from yards running compatible management software, especially their own Checkmate system.

Scale is the whole story here. A buyer looking for a 2017 Ford F-150 tailgate might see results from hundreds of yards across the country. That kind of breadth makes it the default first stop for a lot of parts buyers.

Strengths:
  • 200 million parts — the largest searchable inventory database in the industry by a wide margin
  • Broad geographic reach spanning the entire US and parts of Canada
  • Tight integration with Checkmate yard management software. If you run Checkmate, your inventory flows directly to Car-Part.com
  • High buyer traffic. Many repair shops and body shops search Car-Part.com first
  • Used by both professional yards and retail parts buyers
Weaknesses:
  • Data freshness varies. Inventory records are only as current as the last sync from a yard's management software. A yard that syncs weekly has week-old data
  • Size creates noise. A search can return dozens of results, and the buyer can't tell which listings reflect actual current availability
  • Yards without Checkmate or compatible software are going to struggle keeping listings current
  • Commoditizes parts — your listing sits alongside hundreds of others, competing on price alone
Best for: Yards that want maximum passive reach and are willing to maintain current inventory records. Strong for yards already running Checkmate or compatible management software.

Hotline HQ

Network size: 500+ yards across 12 regional rooms Model: Live voice network (always-on conference bridge)

Hotline HQ works differently from both PartsHotlines and Car-Part.com. No database. No listings. Yards connect a desk phone to an always-on conference room for their region. Someone needs a part, they key up and describe it. Every yard in the room hears the request and responds live if they have it.

Average response time on the network: about 2 seconds. That speed comes from the design itself — you're asking real people in real time, not searching records that may be hours or days old.

Hotline HQ operates in 12 regional markets, with the highest yard density in California, Texas, Arizona, and Florida.

Strengths:
  • Real-time communication. No data staleness — when a yard says "I have it," they're confirming availability right now
  • Average response time of ~2 seconds from broadcast to first answer (Hotline HQ network data)
  • Zero inventory uploading required. No software, no listings, no data entry. Plug in the phone and start
  • Regional rooms group yards by geography, so matches are typically shippable or even drivable
  • Flat monthly pricing with no commissions. The sale is between yards — Hotline HQ doesn't take a cut
  • Surfaces demand you'd never see. You hear requests for parts you didn't know someone needed
Weaknesses:
  • Smaller network than Car-Part.com or PartsHotlines. 500+ yards across 12 rooms vs. thousands in a database
  • Regional coverage only. If your market isn't in one of the 12 active rooms, the network doesn't reach you yet
  • Voice-based means you need someone in the yard listening. Not a passive system you can set and forget
  • No searchable online database for end consumers. This is a yard-to-yard network
Best for: Yards that value speed and accuracy over passive reach. Strong for yards that need to fulfill customer requests fast and want to hear real-time demand from their region.

Side-by-side comparison

PartsHotlines.comCar-Part.comHotline HQ
Network size1,000+ yards200M parts listed500+ yards, 12 rooms
How it worksSearch a curated databaseSearch a massive inventory databaseLive voice broadcast to all connected yards
Speed to first resultSeconds (search), then call to confirmSeconds (search), then call to confirm~2 seconds to a live, confirmed response
Data freshnessDepends on upload frequencyDepends on software sync frequencyReal-time (live voice)
Inventory upload neededYesYes (via software)No
Cost modelMembership feeListing/subscriptionFlat monthly, no commissions
Buyer typeYards, some shopsYards, shops, retail consumersYards only (B2B)
Geographic coverageNationalNational + international12 regional US markets
Best forPassive exposure to verified yardsMaximum reach and buyer trafficSpeed, accuracy, real-time demand

The data freshness problem

This is the core trade-off between database networks and a live voice network. And it hits every search result you see or listing you post.

A salvage yard's inventory changes constantly. Vehicles arrive. Parts get pulled and sold. Cores get crushed. A busy day might mean dozens of parts changing hands. But database syncs run on a schedule — daily if you're diligent, weekly or monthly if you're not.

That gap between reality and records creates stale listings. A buyer finds a 2019 Chevy Silverado door on Car-Part.com, calls the yard, hears "sorry, sold that Tuesday." Wasted time on both ends.

PartsHotlines has the same problem. Even with hand-selected, quality yards, inventory records drift between uploads.

Hotline HQ sidesteps this entirely. When a yard responds to a broadcast on the voice network, they're confirming the part is available right now. No record to go stale because there's no record. The information is live, from a person who can walk the yard and check.

The trade-off is reach. Databases can store millions of parts and serve them to anyone searching at any hour. A voice network only works when yards are connected and listening. Both models carry real value — they just handle freshness differently.

Which network fits your yard?

Choose PartsHotlines if your yard maintains clean inventory records and you want steady, passive exposure to a curated network. The hand-selected membership means your parts are listed alongside other serious, operating yards. Good fit for yards that treat their data as an asset and update it regularly. Choose Car-Part.com if maximum reach matters most. No other network puts your inventory in front of more potential buyers. If you're already running Checkmate, the integration is tight and keeps your listings current with minimal effort. Hard to ignore if you sell to retail consumers and repair shops — not just other yards. The buyer traffic alone makes the case. Choose Hotline HQ if speed and accuracy matter most. Your customer calls needing a part found fast — one broadcast reaches your entire region in seconds. No listing management. No data entry. No stale records. And if you want to know what parts are moving across your region, the hotline tells you in real time. Explore the marketplace to see the network in action.

No wrong answer here. The wrong move is choosing nothing and riding only on walk-in traffic and word of mouth.

Why most yards use more than one

Honest take: these networks aren't mutually exclusive. Plenty of yards run two or even all three.

Car-Part.com gives you broad, passive reach. Your inventory sits in the largest database in the industry, and buyers find it on their own. That's worth something even when the data drifts.

PartsHotlines gives you curated exposure to serious yards. The hand-selected network filters out the noise.

Hotline HQ gives you speed and real-time demand intelligence. You hear what yards in your region need right now. Parts collecting dust on your shelves might be exactly what someone broadcasts for tomorrow morning.

The yards that sell the most parts don't lean on one channel. They combine passive reach (databases) with active selling (voice networks and direct relationships). If you're finding parts online or helping customers track down hard-to-find components, a multi-network approach covers more ground.

Software tracks your inventory. Databases list it. A voice network sells it live. Different tools, different jobs.

A yard running Car-Part.com plus Hotline HQ covers both angles. The database catches buyers searching online at midnight. The voice network catches requests from yards across the state at 10 AM. Neither one replaces the other.

The question isn't which network to pick. It's which combination matches how you run your yard and where your buyers are.

Try a Live Room in Your State

The comparison above is theory; the line itself is practice. Hotline HQ's most active rooms are California, Texas, Florida, and Arizona. Joining is free, and the always-on voice network works from any phone you already own.

posts.title: Salvage Yard Software Compared — Inventory Tools for Auto Dismantlers
posts.description: An honest comparison of the top salvage yard software tools — Dismantly, YardSmart, Frazer, Car-Part Checkmate, and Yard Manager — plus where voice networks fit in.
posts.author: Hotline HQ Team
posts.authorRole: The team behind the largest voice parts network in the US
posts.keywords: salvage yard software, auto dismantler inventory software, junkyard management tools, salvage yard inventory system, dismantler software comparison

toc.label: Why your yard needs inventory software

faq.q: What is the best software for a salvage yard?
faq.a: It depends on your yard's size and goals. Dismantly is strong for yards selling across online marketplaces. YardSmart suits multi-location operations. Frazer is a proven desktop suite for full business management. Car-Part Checkmate connects directly to the Car-Part.com buyer network. Yard Manager covers the basics at a lower price point.

faq.q: Do I need salvage yard software if I use a voice hotline?
faq.a: They solve different problems. Software tracks what you have in inventory. A voice hotline like Hotline HQ connects you with buyers who need parts right now. Most yards that use Hotline HQ also run inventory software — the two work together.

faq.q: How much does salvage yard software cost?
faq.a: Pricing varies widely. Cloud-based tools like Dismantly charge monthly subscriptions, typically starting around $100-300/month depending on features. Frazer and YardSmart use quote-based pricing that factors in yard size and modules. Yard Manager is generally the most affordable option. Most vendors don't publish exact prices — you'll need to request a demo.

posts.bodyHtml:
Why your yard needs inventory software

A salvage yard without inventory software is running blind. You can't sell what you can't find, and you can't price what you haven't tracked.

That was fine when yards were small enough that the owner knew every part on every shelf. Most operations outgrow that fast.

Good salvage yard software handles the core workflow: vehicle acquisition, parts cataloging, interchange lookup, pricing, and sales tracking. Some tools go further — marketplace syndication, accounting, fleet management. The right pick depends on your yard's size, your sales channels, and how much you want to manage from one screen.

Here's an honest look at five tools dismantlers actually use, what each does well, and where each falls short.

Dismantly

What it does: Cloud-based inventory management with AI-powered part identification. Dismantly scans vehicles and suggests parts to pull based on market demand. It syndicates listings across eBay, Facebook Marketplace, and other channels from one dashboard. Who it's for: Small to mid-size yards that sell heavily through online marketplaces and want automated listing management. Pricing model: Monthly subscription, tiered by inventory volume and marketplace integrations. Pros:
  • AI-assisted part identification speeds up cataloging
  • Inspection checklists help standardize part grading across your team
  • Multi-channel listing sync — update once, publish everywhere
  • Cloud-based, so you can access it from any device. No local server needed
  • Modern interface that newer employees pick up quickly
Cons:
  • Newer player compared to the established names
  • Marketplace sync reliability depends on third-party API changes
  • Less suited for yards that do most business through walk-ins or phone calls
  • Feature set is still growing — some advanced reporting isn't there yet

YardSmart

What it does: Multi-yard management platform with Hollander Interchange integration. YardSmart tracks inventory across locations, handles parts interchange lookups, and generates operational reports. Who it's for: Larger operations running two or more yards, or single yards with complex inventory that need deep interchange data. Pricing model: Quote-based. Pricing depends on yard count, user seats, and modules selected. Pros:
  • Hollander Interchange integration means accurate cross-referencing across makes and models
  • Multi-yard support with centralized inventory view
  • Strong reporting for tracking which vehicles and parts generate the most revenue
  • Handles yard-to-yard transfers for multi-location operations
Cons:
  • Steeper learning curve than simpler tools
  • Pricing climbs fast when you start adding yards and modules
  • Overkill for a single small yard that just needs basic tracking
  • Implementation takes longer due to data migration and training

Frazer DMS

What it does: A desktop-based dealer management system covering the full salvage yard workflow — vehicle acquisition, dismantling, inventory, sales, and accounting. Frazer has been in the auto dealer software space for decades. Who it's for: Yards that want one system for everything, accounting and title management included. Strong for yards that also buy and sell whole vehicles. Pricing model: Quote-based with licensing fees. Desktop installation, not cloud. Pros:
  • Covers the entire business cycle from buying vehicles to selling parts to closing the books
  • Proven track record — Frazer has been serving auto dealers and dismantlers for years
  • Integrated accounting means you might not need separate QuickBooks
  • Title and vehicle acquisition management built in
Cons:
  • Desktop-based — no native cloud access from mobile or remote locations
  • Interface shows its age next to newer cloud tools
  • Less focused on marketplace syndication than Dismantly or Checkmate
  • Updates and support tied to licensing terms

Car-Part Checkmate

What it does: Inventory management built by Car-Part.com, the largest used auto parts search engine. Checkmate manages your yard's inventory and pushes it directly to the Car-Part.com network, where millions of searches happen monthly. Who it's for: Yards that want maximum exposure on Car-Part.com's buyer network. If most of your remote sales come through Car-Part.com searches, Checkmate tightens that pipeline. Pricing model: Subscription-based, often bundled with Car-Part.com listing fees. Pros:
  • Direct integration with Car-Part.com's 200-million-part search network
  • Inventory updates flow straight to where buyers are already looking
  • Car-Part.com's interchange data is built in
  • Established ecosystem with a large user base of yards
Cons:
  • Ties your inventory workflow to one marketplace ecosystem
  • Less flexible for yards selling mostly through eBay or Facebook
  • Switching costs are real — migrating away means losing the tight Car-Part.com integration
  • Yard management features are secondary to the marketplace connection

SalvageSoftware Yard Manager

What it does: Parts tracking with pull lists, dismantle lists, and basic inventory management. Yard Manager covers the fundamentals without the overhead of enterprise systems. Who it's for: Smaller yards looking for affordable inventory tracking. A good starting point if you're moving from spreadsheets to real software. Pricing model: Lower-cost subscription or license. Generally the most budget-friendly option in this comparison. Pros:
  • Lower cost of entry than enterprise tools
  • Straightforward interface — less training needed
  • Covers the essentials: parts tracking, pull lists, dismantle scheduling
  • Fits yards that don't need marketplace syndication or multi-yard management
Cons:
  • Feature set is limited compared to Dismantly, YardSmart, or Frazer
  • Fewer integrations with external marketplaces and interchange databases
  • May not scale well as your operation grows
  • Reporting and analytics are basic

Feature comparison table

FeatureDismantlyYardSmartFrazer DMSCheckmateYard Manager

Multi-yard supportLimitedYesYesYesNo
Hollander InterchangePartialYesYesYesNo
Marketplace sync (eBay, FB)YesLimitedNoNoNo
